title: Talking about my life - Austin, Texas 1/21

transcript.text: Hello, I'm going to talk about my life.
I got the inspiration to this, I'm reading a book, Many Lives, Many Masters, by Brian Weiss,
And this guy is hypnotizing this woman into talking about her past life, so I'm going to talk about my life.
So, the earliest thing that I remember routinely, I don't, I don't, I'm not,
One of these people who remembers back, you know, when I'm like three,
Like, my mother's brother says he remembers his first birthday and blowing out the cake,
I can't go that far. When I was about three or four, we moved from, I was born in Austin, Texas,
We moved from like this house on Rock Creek Drive, a couple blocks from the river,
Up into West Lake Hills, and I don't remember living in the Rock Creek house.
I've seen pictures of it, me as a baby running around in the pool and stuff,
But I don't remember it. And I don't even remember, well, I think I vaguely remember
Living in the West Lake Hills house and knowing that we hadn't lived there for very long,
We just moved there. But, I mean, I don't really, I didn't even remember that.
All I remember is the West Lake Hills house. So anyways, we had this West Lake Hills house.
We were neighbors with, you know, the governor, or future governor, Ann, what was her name,
Ann Richards, and her daughter was my babysitter. And I do remember having a babysitter there,
Um, who like, was this girl who was fascinated by our TV remote control, and she was like seeing
How far away she could stand away from the TV and the remote would still work. And she was like way
Over on the other side of the hallway from my parents' room, seeing if it worked and stuff.
And my mom said there was a girl who was my babysitter who thought that I was English.
She thought I was adopted because I spoke in an English accent when I was a kid.
But, um, but yeah, we had this house, like, and my brother and I shared a room, even though
There were two rooms in the basement for some reason, my brother and I shared a room. And
I remember when he was really young, before he, he couldn't remember what happened the
Day before. So I would like hit him and he'd cry. And then the next day he'd like want to
Be my best friend. And he like, wouldn't remember like me hitting him the day before. So I would
Like hit him and he'd cry and like, I wouldn't, and then he'd be friends the next day because
He wouldn't remember what happened the day before. Like, yeah, we were like, I remember
Like running around, um, uh, from the kitchen to the living room, the divider thing, doing
Laps, running around with him. And I'd be like lapping him and he'd keep running him. Um,
And, um, we had a pool and you could see the skyline of Austin from the house, but I don't
Remember seeing it because we had a fence there and I wasn't tall enough to look over
The fence. Um, and we lived on the side of a hill and, um, on the top of the hill actually.
And we, and we'd walk down the hill through a forest that was part of our land to a tent
Platform. And I don't ever recall actually camping on that tent or maybe once, but we
Would just go down there and do yoga with my mom. And as far as I could tell, I never
Saw the boundaries of our, of our land. I did go back there a few years ago and I walked
Down. It wasn't so much farther down from the, uh, for the tent, from the tent, um,
Found a platform where it was the bottom of the goalie. But, um, yeah, we had these two
Like four wheeler pedal bike things. One of them was a Jeep, a world war two Jeep. And
The other one was the Batmobile that we'd ride around. But I got bored with those pretty quick.
I wanted to learn how to ride a bike, but the problem was the road out in front of our
House was a dirt road with lots of potholes. And, um, so it was hard for me to, you know,
The wheels, I couldn't get up to speed. And when my, and my father, so I rode around on
Training wheels, but I didn't want to ride training wheels. I wanted to ride the bike
Without the training wheels. So every, every weekend, my father would take the, uh,
Training wheels off and he would hold the bike up for me, but he wouldn't teach me how to ride
A bike by pushing me for some reason. He didn't push me. So I couldn't get up the motivation,
The momentum. So I would have to, he would just let go of the bike and I'd have to ride it.
Um, and you know, I'd have to get the moment of myself riding over the potholes in the dirt. So
It took me a while, but I finally realized, I finally was able to do it when I was four. I was
Able to ride a bike, a bike, but I learned, but you know, I could ride on like a dirt road,
But I just rode around the house. I didn't go on like long rides because like we lived kind of
Out of town on dirt roads. There was this, um, there was like, we, we kind of the road that our
House is on, it went down to a dirt, a dead end. And I went there a couple of years ago and the
Dead end still exists. And there were no houses there. It was just this like parking lot that
You could overlook the city. And so I'd ride my bike back and forth to there, which was like a
Couple with like a hundred or a couple hundred yards or something with my brother. We would go
Down there a lot. And we, there were daisies there. Cause I remember thinking, Oh look, daisies,
Daisy flowers. These are daisies. Just like the name of the girl from Dukes of Hazzard. Cause
Her name's Daisy. Because we watched Dukes of Hazzard came on after the incredible Hulk. We'd
Watch incredible Hulk. Oh no, no. Dukes of Hazzard was first and then the incredible Hulk. So we'd
Watch both of those shows. Those are like the two shows we watched. And we had a dog
And a cat, Luis and Biscuit. Uh, we found Biscuit as a, um, as a, uh, stray dog. He just
Like followed us and we picked him up. One time he disappeared for like a week and a friend
Of the family saw him at a gas station in town, which is like a long ways from our house.
So they put him in the car and brought us back, brought him back to the house. But
Every time we left, he would run after the car barking for like the longest time for like
A couple blocks. Every single time he'd run after the car barking. And, uh, yeah, we had
A neighbor. The, uh, another next door neighbor of ours was Stevie Ray Vaughan. I didn't know
That at the time. I went and visited the house we grew up and they, they actually told us
That. No, no, it wasn't Stevie Ray Vaughan. It was Stevie Ray Vaughan's brother. But, um,
Anyways, um, yeah, there was, there was supposed to be, there was this crazy dog there who like
A neighbor had a crazy dog who like killed a dog and a cat. I remember, but, um,
There was, there was another kid who lived across the street, but I didn't hang out with him. I
Just remember hanging out in front of his house one day and my mom was like, Oh, you're the oldest
Too. Kyle's the oldest. How'd you like being the oldest? Yeah, I like being the oldest. And that's
All I remember. Oh, and he had this like, he had this like, uh, three wheeler that he would like
Go down his really steep driveway and pull the thing and like slide. So it's like, cool. Um,
And I remember once my dad went and bought me a bigger bike, BMX bike. We drove way out to some
House and he got it used from somebody. And I rode around on that a little bit, but the main bike I
Had for the longest, that was like not long before we moved to Boulder. So the main bike I had was this
Little red bike, but I outgrew that, you know, that's why he bought me that other bike. But
I didn't really ride it. It had a flat tire when he bought it and he didn't like fix it
Immediately. So I kind of didn't really. And when I finally got it fixed, I don't know.
I didn't really ride that one as much, but, um, I went to a, I remember the school. I went
To the preschool. Hey, I remember the preschool I went to, um, called Lampin. No, no, no,
No. What was it called? Oh my God. I can't remember. The kindergarten was called Lampin
Lighter. The preschool was Montessori and my mom would drive us through the forest really
Far. It was like way out of town and it was this one building. And, uh, on the other side
Was my brother's area for the younger kids. And then my side was for the older kids.
And, um, we just played, I don't remember. We had different schedules or whatever. The
Breath, my brother's side was more fun cause there were more toys and stuff. And sometimes
I would sneak over there and play over there. Um, but I remember standing outside of the, you
Know, at like lunch and we would have like lunch with the kids, other kids. One of the
Kids had a, uh, one of the kids had a, um, R2-D2 from Star Wars. And, um, my parents said
That we saw Star Wars in the movie theater in Fort Collins, but I don't remember that.
But, uh, he had an R2-D2. And we had lunch and the other kids would have like white Wonder
Bread, uh, peanut butter jelly sandwiches and like potato chips and stuff. But my, my breakfast,
My mom gave me like chunky peanut butter on like rice cakes. And like, it was like the
Health food giving me like health food. And other kids would have like, you know, like fruit
Juice or whatever. And I kind of felt like that was cool. Like I was healthier cause I
Had the health food lunches. Um, I remember when my mom picked me up every day after school
Cause we drive around this, like it had this like big, like loopty doopty thing around the
Building. So I remember just driving around that before we leave and their whole green,
Lime green, Osmobile, um, station wagon. Um, I remember once my grandmother, my mother's
Mother visited and she was like, yeah, look at the car. Like you can, you know, move the
Seat down and have like the bed area. And then you can open up the door this way or the other
Way. It was like right after she got it. And my dad had a black Osmobile, uh, Toronado.
And I remember him saying, it was like really strong. Like it was faster than the Trans Am
And just as fast as the Corvette. And I was like, cool. And I'd be like telling my friends
That, you know, but like, um, I think I had a friend, I had a friend, a best friend. I think
It was from that school named Patrick Dickerson. And, uh, anyways, yeah, when I was at that school,
Montessori, I remember once we had a, um, Christmas party at that place and my parents
Were said, Oh yeah, Santa Claus is on the roof right now giving, um, give it presents and
Stuff. And I actually believed them, but I remember another time we were in town and we
Were sitting on Santa's lap and he was like saying, Oh, Oh, Oh, Oh, what do you want for
Breakfast for Christmas? And this kid who was with me was like, look, that's not Santa. Cause
He was like behind the guy's head was like the guy's real hair. And I was like, Oh yeah,
Look, it's not Santa. But let's pretend we think it's Santa. But, um, but, uh, I don't know
If I believed there was a Santa at that point, but, um, yeah, but there we, I remember at one
Point I had, um, I had, um, diarrhea and the lady took me upstairs and like took all my clothes
Off and put me in a bathtub to wash me off. And the bathtub was like, got all like shitty.
But then like, and then my mom picked me up early because I had the diarrhea. Um, and
Then when I was, I don't know how old I was, I went and changed schools to this other school
Called lamp and lighter. That was, I guess it was the kindergarten. Um, which is like this
Big complex had lots of, and it was more in town, had lots of schools near it. And there
Was even a high school right next to it. And, um, we had this woman teacher who was nice
And like the kids really liked. And, uh, we did stuff like learning alphabets and stuff
Like that. And I remember I made an E and it was a perfect E and I was like, my God, it's
The perfect E lowercase E. I think my best friend there, Patrick Dickerson was in my class, but
Then he was moved to the other class. And I remember like, we would like, you know, when
We would go off to like other places walking around, we'd be like, step on a crack and break
Your mother's back. So we wouldn't step on the crack. And I jump over the crack. Oh, whoops,
That's done with that part.
